cases.name: State v. Bingham
cases.holding: Having time to deliberate ≠ actually deliberating; must prove defendant in fact deliberated

cases.name: State v. Brown
cases.holding: Deliberation means measuring and evaluating major facets of a choice; weighing reasons for and against killing

cases.name: Gilbert v. State
cases.holding: Factors supporting premeditation include having weapon ready, considering alternatives, positioning to avoid detection, returning for additional weapon

cases.name: People v. Berry
cases.holding: Modern approach to voluntary manslaughter recognizes cumulative passion; 20 hours can still be 'heat of passion'

cases.name: State v. Dumlao
cases.holding: EMED two-step test: (1) view defendant's subjective situation and external circumstances as perceived; (2) assess whether explanation for disturbance was reasonable

cases.name: People v. Knoller
cases.holding: Depraved heart murder requires conscious disregard of risk of DEATH specifically; awareness of serious bodily injury is insufficient

cases.name: State v. Williams
cases.holding: Legislature changed standard from gross negligence to ordinary negligence; reasonable person standard does not incorporate defendant's characteristics

cases.name: People v. James
cases.holding: 'In the abstract' approach: look at statutory elements only to determine if felony is inherently dangerous; can felony be committed without creating substantial risk of death?

cases.name: People v. Bodely
cases.holding: Temporary safety rule: felony not complete until robber reaches place of safety; temporal-spatial and causal link between felonious acts and homicide required

cases.name: Rose v. State
cases.holding: Merger doctrine: underlying felony must be independent of the homicide; assaultive felonies merge with the homicide

cases.name: State v. Canola
cases.holding: Agency rule: felony murder does not extend to killings by third parties (non-co-felons); competing proximate cause theory applies FM to any proximately caused death

cases.name: People v. Atkins
cases.holding: General vs. specific intent classification: look for additional intent language beyond the actus reus to classify the crime
cases.topic: Mens Rea - General vs. Specific Intent

cases.name: State v. Freeman
cases.holding: Mistake of fact analysis framework: specific intent requires honest mistake; general intent requires honest AND reasonable mistake

cases.name: Morissette v. United States
cases.holding: Presumption favoring mens rea in criminal offenses; government bears burden of showing strict liability was intended

cases.name: United States v. Jewell
cases.holding: Willful blindness satisfies 'knowingly' when there is conscious purpose to avoid learning the truth

cases.name: People v. Scott
cases.holding: Transferred intent applies to bad-aim killings where defendant intends to kill one person but kills another

cases.name: State v. Fugate
cases.holding: Intent to kill can be inferred from natural and probable consequences of defendant's act

cases.name: Robinson v. California
cases.holding: Cannot punish status or condition; criminal law punishes only voluntary acts

cases.name: Commonwealth v. Mercado
cases.holding: Involuntary acts (reflexes, convulsions, unconsciousness) do not satisfy actus reus requirement

cases.name: Cheek v. United States
cases.holding: Honest belief negates willfulness in 'willfulness' crimes; but disagreement with the law does not

cases.name: United States v. Yermian
cases.holding: Jurisdictional elements need not have mental state applied; MPC § 2.02 exceptions for jurisdictional facts

cases.name: State v. Barbour
cases.holding: Larceny by trick: possession obtained through fraud/deception; owner retains title

cases.name: State v. Pierce
cases.holding: False pretenses: both title AND possession obtained through fraud; victim transfers ownership

cases.name: People v. Navarro
cases.holding: Theft classification depends on whether fraud obtains possession only (larceny by trick) or title+possession (false pretenses)

cases.name: Regina v. Dudley & Stephens
cases.holding: Necessity cannot justify killing an innocent person; starving sailors cannot kill a cabin boy to survive

cases.name: State v. Arrington
cases.holding: Second-degree murder instruction required when evidence supports premeditation but facts permit jury to find malice without P&D

cases.name: United States v. Bronstein
cases.holding: Void for vagueness: criminal statute unconstitutional if fails to give fair notice or encourages arbitrary enforcement

cases.name: Commonwealth v. Mills
cases.holding: False pretenses: purposely made false statements, knew they were false, intended the city to rely on them, and city did rely — distinguishable from larceny (no trespassory taking) and embezzlement (no trust relationship)

cases.name: State v. Steele
cases.holding: Embezzlement conviction upheld: fiduciary duty can arise from special confidence, not just formal power of attorney; must look at depositor's intent — money entrusted for management, not as gift

cases.name: United States v. Eichman
cases.holding: Entry element of burglary requires intrusion within the four walls or beneath the roof; curtilage expands dwelling house concept to include nearby outbuildings

cases.name: State v. Thibeault
cases.holding: Breaking element requires knowing lack of license/privilege to enter; consent is complete defense to burglary; intent to steal does not negate permission to enter — two separate elements

cases.name: Crocker v. State
cases.holding: Snatching money without force, threat of force, or fear satisfies larceny but not robbery; must prove force or coercion to elevate theft to robbery

cases.name: Miller v. Superior Court
cases.holding: Forcibly preventing victim from regaining control of property during asportation phase equals taking from immediate presence; robbery's 'immediate presence' satisfied during or after carrying away

cases.name: People v. Goetz
cases.holding: Self-defense requires objectively reasonable belief in necessity of force; 'reasonably' modifies 'believes' making it objective standard; but jury may consider defendant's knowledge of victim and prior experiences

cases.name: State v. Simon
cases.holding: Belief in necessity of self-defense must be reasonable to a reasonable person, not just the defendant; prior dealings with victim and mental health conditions relevant to subjective belief but tested objectively

cases.name: United States v. Cole
cases.holding: Securities fraud prosecution of corporate insider who manipulated financial records — illustrates introductory concepts in criminal law and federal criminal jurisdiction
